category: ERP Selling, Buying and Stock Production

items.title: Model the selling pipeline as a forward document chain
items.description: Generate the selling flow as a chain where each document optionally derives from the previous: Lead to Opportunity to Quotation to Sales Order to Delivery Note and Sales Invoice. Downstream documents copy items and carry a back-reference, so quantities and amounts can be tracked against the source.
items.applicability: Pre-sales and order-to-cash modules of a generated ERP.
items.example: A Sales Order created 'from' a Quotation copies its lines and stores the quotation reference; the quotation shows it as converted.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: store an explicit source-document reference per line, not just per header, so partial conversions are traceable. Test implication: assert a derived document copies lines and links back. The chain is optional; ad-hoc direct invoices must still be allowed.

items.title: Lead and opportunity are non-financial CRM stages
items.description: Leads and opportunities track prospective demand and must not post any GL or stock impact. They carry a status lifecycle (e.g. Open, Qualified, Converted, Lost) and convert into a Customer plus a Quotation when qualified.
items.applicability: CRM front of the selling pipeline in a generated ERP.
items.example: A Lead 'Open' becomes 'Qualified', then conversion creates a Customer record and an initial Quotation, marking the Lead 'Converted'.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep these entities free of ledger or ledger references; they are operational, not accounting, records. Test implication: assert converting a lead creates a customer and never writes GL or stock rows. Lead-scoring and campaign attribution are separate optional features.

items.title: Customer master carries billing identity and defaults
items.description: A Customer is a master entity holding billing name, tax identity, default currency, price list, payment terms, and credit limit. Sales documents reference the customer and inherit its defaults, which remain overridable per document.
items.applicability: All selling documents in a generated ERP.
items.example: Creating a Sales Order for ACME defaults the currency and 30-day terms from the customer, while still allowing a per-order override.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: snapshot the billing/tax identity onto the posted invoice so later master edits do not rewrite historical documents. Test implication: assert defaults inherit and that editing the master does not alter a submitted invoice. Customer-group pricing hierarchies are an optional extension.

items.title: Quotation is a non-binding offer with validity
items.description: A Quotation lists items, prices and a validity date and produces no financial or stock impact. After expiry or rejection it cannot be converted; a valid, accepted quotation converts to a Sales Order.
items.applicability: Pre-sales documents in a generated ERP.
items.example: Quotation valid to 2026-06-30 is accepted on 2026-06-25 and converted to a Sales Order; the same quotation cannot convert on 2026-07-02.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: enforce the validity check at conversion time, not just display. Test implication: assert an expired quotation refuses conversion. Versioned re-quotes and approval of discounts on quotations are optional features.

items.title: Sales order is the commitment that tracks fulfilment
items.description: A Sales Order is the customer's confirmed commitment. It tracks ordered quantity against delivered and billed quantities per line, so the system knows what remains to deliver and to invoice. It does not itself post stock or GL.
items.applicability: Order management in a generated ERP.
items.example: SO line ordered 10; after a Delivery Note of 6 the line shows delivered 6, pending 4; the order is 'To Deliver and Bill' until both reach 10.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: maintain delivered/billed running totals atomically as child documents submit and cancel. Test implication: assert pending quantities recompute correctly across partial deliveries and cancellations. Drop-ship and made-to-order variants are separate flows.

items.title: Delivery note posts the outbound stock movement
items.description: A Delivery Note (or the delivering Sales Invoice when stock is updated on invoice) reduces warehouse stock by writing negative stock-ledger entries at the item's current valuation. It is the point where inventory leaves the company.
items.applicability: Shipping and dispatch in a generated ERP with perpetual inventory.
items.example: Delivering 6 units writes a stock-ledger issue of -6 from the source warehouse and posts the cost-of-goods-sold journal at valuation.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: post the stock movement and its COGS GL impact in one transaction so they cannot diverge. Test implication: assert stock decreases by exactly the delivered quantity and COGS equals quantity times valuation. Whether stock updates on delivery or on invoice is a configuration the generator must surface.

items.title: Sales invoice posts receivable and revenue
items.description: A Sales Invoice is the financial selling document: on submit it debits the AR control account for the gross and credits revenue and tax accounts. It tracks billed quantity against the sales order and outstanding against receipts.
items.applicability: Billing in a generated ERP.
items.example: Invoice net 850 + tax 150 debits AR-control 1000, credits Sales Revenue 850 and Output Tax 150; outstanding starts at 1000.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: reuse the accounting posting-template and balance check rather than re-implementing GL logic in the selling module. Test implication: assert the generated GL lines balance and outstanding reduces as receipts allocate. Recurring and proforma invoices are separate document subtypes.

items.title: Payment receipt clears customer outstanding
items.description: A customer Payment Receipt debits a bank/cash account and credits the AR control account, allocating the received amount to specific outstanding invoices. The sum of allocations plus any retained advance equals the receipt amount.
items.applicability: Cash collection in a generated ERP.
items.example: Receipt 1000 allocated 1000 to INV-1 sets INV-1 outstanding to zero and reduces AR-control by 1000.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: reuse the accounting payment-entry allocation logic and forbid over-allocation. Test implication: assert outstanding reaches zero only when fully allocated and that over-allocation is rejected. Auto-application of advances is an explicit option, not a default.

items.title: Credit note reverses a sale by issuing a negative invoice
items.description: A return or correction is handled by a Credit Note (return Sales Invoice) that reverses the original GL and, if goods are returned, posts an inbound stock movement. The original invoice is never edited; the credit note references it.
items.applicability: Sales returns and corrections in a generated ERP.
items.example: Returning 2 of 6 units creates a credit note that credits AR-control, debits revenue and tax for the returned value, and writes a +2 stock receipt.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: link the credit note to the source invoice and cap the returned quantity at what was delivered. Test implication: assert the net of invoice plus credit note reconciles and stock returns to the warehouse. Restocking fees and scrap-on-return are explicit policy add-ons.

items.title: Model the buying pipeline as a forward document chain
items.description: Generate the buying flow as a chain: Supplier and (optionally) a Material Request to Purchase Order to Purchase Receipt and Purchase Invoice. Downstream documents copy items and carry a back-reference enabling a three-way match of order, receipt, and invoice.
items.applicability: Procure-to-pay modules of a generated ERP.
items.example: A Purchase Receipt created from a Purchase Order copies its lines; the Purchase Invoice then matches both for quantity and price before posting.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep per-line references so partial receipts and partial billing are traceable. Test implication: assert a three-way mismatch (e.g. invoice qty > received qty) can be detected. Procurement approval routing is covered by the workflow pack, not here.

items.title: Supplier master carries remittance identity and defaults
items.description: A Supplier master holds legal name, tax identity, default currency, payment terms, and bank details for remittance. Purchase documents reference the supplier and inherit its defaults, overridable per document.
items.applicability: All buying documents in a generated ERP.
items.example: A Purchase Order defaults the supplier's currency and 45-day terms; the eventual supplier payment uses the stored remittance bank details.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: snapshot tax identity onto the posted purchase invoice so later master edits do not alter history. Test implication: assert defaults inherit and submitted documents are unaffected by master edits. Supplier scorecards and onboarding workflows are optional extensions.

items.title: Purchase order is the buyer commitment that tracks receipt and billing
items.description: A Purchase Order is the confirmed commitment to a supplier. It tracks ordered quantity against received and billed quantities per line and posts no stock or GL impact itself. It is the anchor for the three-way match.
items.applicability: Procurement in a generated ERP.
items.example: PO line ordered 100; a Purchase Receipt of 40 leaves pending 60; the PO stays 'To Receive and Bill' until received and billed both reach 100.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: maintain received/billed running totals atomically as child documents submit and cancel. Test implication: assert pending quantities recompute across partial receipts. Blanket and scheduled orders are separate subtypes.

items.title: Purchase receipt posts the inbound stock movement
items.description: A Purchase Receipt increases warehouse stock by writing positive stock-ledger entries at the purchase rate and updates the item's valuation. It debits a stock-in-hand asset and credits a goods-received-not-invoiced clearing account pending the invoice.
items.applicability: Goods receiving in a generated ERP with perpetual inventory.
items.example: Receiving 40 at 25 writes a +40 stock-ledger entry, increases stock value by 1000, debits Stock-in-Hand 1000 and credits GRNI 1000.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: use a goods-received-not-invoiced clearing account so receipt and invoice can occur in either order without double counting. Test implication: assert stock increases by received quantity and the clearing account nets to zero after the matching invoice. Quality-inspection holds are an optional gate.

items.title: Purchase invoice posts payable and clears GRNI
items.description: A Purchase Invoice is the financial buying document: it credits the AP control account for the gross and debits expense or the GRNI clearing account plus input tax. Matched against the receipt, it clears the goods-received-not-invoiced balance.
items.applicability: Vendor billing in a generated ERP.
items.example: Invoice for the 40 units debits GRNI 1000 and Input Tax 180, credits AP-control 1180; GRNI returns to zero for that receipt.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: reuse the accounting posting-template and balance check; do not re-implement GL in the buying module. Test implication: assert GRNI clears and AP outstanding equals the gross. Price-variance handling when invoice rate differs from receipt rate is an explicit feature to add.

items.title: Supplier payment clears payable outstanding
items.description: A Supplier Payment debits the AP control account and credits a bank/cash account, allocating the paid amount to specific outstanding purchase invoices. The sum of allocations plus any advance equals the payment amount.
items.applicability: Vendor disbursement in a generated ERP.
items.example: Paying 1180 allocated fully to BILL-1 sets its outstanding to zero and reduces AP-control by 1180.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: reuse the accounting allocation logic and forbid over-allocation. Test implication: assert outstanding clears only on full allocation and over-allocation is rejected. Payment batching and remittance advice are optional features.

items.title: Debit note reverses a purchase by issuing a negative invoice
items.description: A purchase return or correction is handled by a Debit Note (return Purchase Invoice) that reverses the original GL and, if goods are returned, posts an outbound stock movement. The original invoice is never edited; the debit note references it.
items.applicability: Purchase returns and corrections in a generated ERP.
items.example: Returning 10 of 40 units creates a debit note that debits AP-control, credits GRNI/expense and input tax for the returned value, and writes a -10 stock issue.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: link to the source invoice and cap the returned quantity at what was received. Test implication: assert the net of invoice plus debit note reconciles and stock leaves the warehouse. Supplier credit-balance carry-forward is an explicit policy.

items.title: Item master defines stock behaviour and defaults
items.description: An Item master carries a unique code, name, item group, stock unit of measure, an is-stock-item flag, valuation method, and default warehouse. Non-stock items (services) skip stock-ledger postings entirely. Selling and buying documents reference items and inherit their defaults.
items.applicability: All inventory, selling and buying documents in a generated ERP.
items.example: A service item flagged non-stock appears on invoices but never writes a stock-ledger entry; a stock item posts movements on receipt and delivery.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: gate all stock-ledger writes on the is-stock-item flag so services cannot create phantom inventory. Test implication: assert a non-stock item produces no stock-ledger rows. Variants, batches, and serials are explicit extensions, not assumed.

items.title: Item group is a hierarchy for classification and defaults
items.description: Item Group is a tree used to classify items and to provide cascading defaults (default accounts, tax templates). Reports and filters use the group hierarchy; groups themselves are not stocked.
items.applicability: Item classification and default resolution in a generated ERP.
items.example: Group 'Raw Materials' sets a default expense account inherited by its items unless the item overrides it.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: resolve defaults by walking up the group tree with the item-level value winning. Test implication: assert default resolution picks the nearest defined ancestor. Group-level pricing rules are an optional feature.

items.title: Warehouse scopes stock balances
items.description: A Warehouse is the location dimension of stock; every stock-ledger entry and balance is keyed by item and warehouse. Balances and valuation are computed per item-warehouse, never globally per item only.
items.applicability: All stock movements and balance reports in a generated ERP.
items.example: Item A holds 10 in Main and 4 in Branch; a transfer of 3 moves the balance without changing the company-wide total of 14.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: index stock entries by (item, warehouse) and compute balances per pair. Test implication: assert per-warehouse balances sum to the company total and a transfer is balance-neutral overall. Bin-level and rack-level location is a finer extension.

items.title: Stock ledger is append-only and immutable
items.description: Every stock movement writes an immutable StockLedgerEntry (item, warehouse, signed quantity, valuation rate, posting timestamp, voucher reference). Entries are never edited or deleted; corrections are made by posting reversing entries. The current balance is the running sum of entries.
items.applicability: The integrity backbone of any perpetual-inventory generated ERP.
items.example: Receipt +40, issue -6, transfer-out -3 produce three immutable rows; the balance 31 is their signed sum, fully reconstructable.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: forbid update and delete on the ledger table at the repository level; only inserts are allowed. Test implication: assert no code path mutates an existing entry and that balance equals the sum of entries. Reconstruction performance for very long histories may need periodic balance snapshots, a deliberate optimisation.

items.title: Guard against negative stock by default
items.description: An issue, delivery, or transfer-out must be rejected when it would drive the item-warehouse balance below zero, unless negative stock is explicitly enabled by configuration. The check uses the projected balance at the posting moment, computed under a transaction.
items.applicability: All outbound stock postings in a generated ERP.
items.example: With 5 on hand, an attempt to deliver 6 is rejected with a domain error; delivering 5 succeeds and leaves zero.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: compute the projected balance and enforce the guard inside the same transaction as the ledger insert to avoid races. Test implication: concurrent issues must not jointly oversell; assert rejection at the boundary. Allowing negative stock is a deliberate, dangerous opt-in the generator must surface, not enable silently.

items.title: Model receipt, issue, transfer and adjustment as stock entry types
items.description: A single Stock Entry document with a purpose (Receipt, Issue, Transfer, Adjustment) covers manual movements: Receipt adds to one warehouse, Issue removes from one, Transfer removes from a source and adds to a target in one balanced document, and Adjustment corrects to a counted quantity.
items.applicability: Manual and inter-warehouse inventory operations in a generated ERP.
items.example: A Transfer of 3 from Main to Branch writes -3 at Main and +3 at Branch in one document; cancelling it reverses both legs.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: a transfer must write both legs atomically so a partial transfer cannot occur. Test implication: assert a transfer is balance-neutral overall and a cancel reverses both legs. Manufacturing consume/produce entries are a distinct, separate document.

items.title: Stock adjustment reconciles book stock to a physical count
items.description: A stock reconciliation/adjustment sets the on-hand quantity (and optionally valuation) of items to counted figures, posting the difference as a ledger entry and a corresponding GL gain or loss. It never silently overwrites the ledger.
items.applicability: Periodic stock-take and shrinkage handling in a generated ERP.
items.example: Book shows 31, count shows 29; the adjustment writes a -2 ledger entry and posts a stock-loss expense of 2 times valuation.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: derive the delta from current balance and post it as a normal immutable ledger entry, not a balance overwrite. Test implication: assert the post-adjustment balance equals the count and a GL gain/loss is recorded. Cycle-count scheduling is an optional feature.

items.title: Value stock by a declared, consistent method
items.description: Each item declares a valuation method (e.g. Moving Average or FIFO) and the generator must apply it consistently when computing the issue rate and remaining value. Inbound entries set the rate; outbound entries draw the method's current rate; the ledger stores the rate used on each entry.
items.applicability: Cost-of-goods-sold and inventory-valuation reporting in a generated ERP.
items.example: Moving average: receive 10 at 20 then 10 at 30 gives an average of 25; issuing 5 books COGS at 25 each and leaves 15 valued at 25.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: store the valuation rate on each ledger entry so historical valuation is reconstructable and recompute the running rate on every inbound under transaction. Test implication: assert moving-average and FIFO produce the documented COGS for a known sequence. Mixing methods for one item, or retroactive back-dated entries, require careful re-valuation logic that must be added deliberately.

items.title: Compute stock balance as a projected running sum
items.description: The stock-balance report aggregates signed ledger quantities and values per item-warehouse up to a date, giving on-hand quantity, valuation rate, and stock value. It is computed, never stored as an editable field that could drift from the ledger.
items.applicability: Inventory reporting and availability checks in a generated ERP.
items.example: Balance as of 2026-06-21 for Item A in Main sums all entries to that date: +40 -6 -3 = 31 at the prevailing valuation.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: build the report from per-item-warehouse aggregation with date filtering; in this codebase avoid group-by-constant projections known to hang SQL Server, and project to a lightweight row. Test implication: assert the reported balance equals the ledger sum at the date. A cached balance snapshot is an optional performance feature that must reconcile to the ledger.

items.title: Generate a reorder skeleton, not an automatic purchase
items.description: Items may declare a reorder level and reorder quantity per warehouse. The generator emits a skeleton that flags items whose projected balance falls below the reorder level and proposes a draft material request or purchase order for human review. It must not auto-submit purchases.
items.applicability: Replenishment assistance in a generated ERP.
items.example: Item A reorder level 10, on hand 8: the reorder report flags it and drafts a material request for the reorder quantity, awaiting approval.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: produce drafts only; submission goes through the normal approval workflow. Test implication: assert below-level items are flagged and that no purchasing document is auto-submitted. Demand forecasting and lead-time-aware reorder points are out of scope and would need separate validation before trust.
